claim_under_attack: Bayer should not break up immediately because current debt, litigation, and weak earnings make a near-term separation more likely to destroy value than unlock it.
evidence_or_reason: Side B asserts that a near-term breakup would be 'more likely to be a distressed restructuring than a clean value-unlocking separation,' but provides no mechanism or precedent showing that debt levels of EUR34.5B and unresolved litigation actually preclude a structured partial separation. The claim treats breakup as binary (full immediate split vs. full delay) while ignoring that a targeted divestiture of the lower-litigation-risk consumer health segment — which carries minimal Monsanto exposure — could reduce debt and conglomerate discount simultaneously without triggering the creditor and litigation pricing risks Side B identifies. Side B cites no evidence that creditors or counterparties would actually reprice worse under a partial separation today versus in 24-36 months, nor does it quantify the incremental value destruction from continued conglomerate structure during the delay window. The 'fix first' logic assumes operating improvement is achievable and likely; given that 2024 EBITDA guidance was already lower than 2023, Side B's confidence that conditions will be materially better in 24-36 months is an assertion, not a demonstrated probability.
